messages.author: ozcleric
messages.text: Hello Sydney! The default LoRa settings for Australia are 915.8 MHz SF 10 CR 5 BW 250Khz. Edit August 2025 Due to the long links we are trying to achieve especially in Western Sydney the repeaters going up out here are running on SF11 (edited)
messages.content_html: Hello Sydney! The default LoRa settings for Australia are 915.8 MHz SF 10 CR 5 BW 250Khz. Edit August 2025 Due to the long links we are trying to achieve especially in Western Sydney the repeaters going up out here are running on SF11 (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 2

messages.author: ozcleric
messages.text: In Sydney just flashed my RAK4631 solar MT node OTA to a MC Repeater - for everyone from ANZ remember you have to change the freq in the platformio.ini BEFORE you run "pio run -e RAK_4631_Repeater" and then you can do the OTA update from MT to MC
messages.content_html: In Sydney just flashed my RAK4631 solar MT node OTA to a MC Repeater - for everyone from ANZ remember you have to change the freq in the platformio.ini BEFORE you run "pio run -e RAK_4631_Repeater" and then you can do the OTA update from MT to MC
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: pacjunk
messages.text: As I've mentioned before, you cant really look at SNR figures as the range of possible SNRs change with spreading factor. Lowest reliable SNR for SF10 is -15. Lowest for SF7 is -7.5. https://www.thethingsnetwork.org/docs/lorawan/rssi-and-snr/snr-demodulation-limits.png On SF10 you won't see more than about +6, but with SF7, you will see much higher. The only way to really tell is to try it - which you have done. If SF7/8 improves the link reliability, then this is all that matters really. Given some of the experience in Sydney where they found SF11 was better, they may find SF8 is better than SF7. Only way to tell is to try it.
messages.content_html: As I've mentioned before, you cant really look at SNR figures as the range of possible SNRs change with spreading factor. Lowest reliable SNR for SF10 is -15. Lowest for SF7 is -7.5. https://www.thethingsnetwork.org/docs/lorawan/rssi-and-snr/snr-demodulation-limits.png On SF10 you won't see more than about +6, but with SF7, you will see much higher. The only way to really tell is to try it - which you have done. If SF7/8 improves the link reliability, then this is all that matters really. Given some of the experience in Sydney where they found SF11 was better, they may find SF8 is better than SF7. Only way to tell is to try it.

messages.reply_html:
che aporeps 🌮
have a look at the SNRs and the distance on this neighbours list

messages.author: pacjunk
messages.text: That's why you reduce the bandwidth at the same time, as this brings the sensitivity back up. Still haven't seen a good explanation of advantages of wide vs narrow. Have a play with this: https://unsigned.io/understanding-lora-parameters/
messages.content_html: That's why you reduce the bandwidth at the same time, as this brings the sensitivity back up. Still haven't seen a good explanation of advantages of wide vs narrow. Have a play with this: https://unsigned.io/understanding-lora-parameters/
embeds_html:
LoRa bitrate calculator and understanding LoRa parameters
It can be pretty useful to know how LoRa parameters influence data rate and range, for example when configuring your RNode. In this post I'll go over the basic concepts needed to understand the different LoRa parameters, and how they affect range and data rate.
messages.reactions_html: 1
messages.reply_html:
Soupbones(VK2FRT)
As for Sydney using SF11, we have to for Range. Sydney Metro area is at least 5000squre KM bigger than Melb and currently we have a huge gap in the middle of syd with no meshcore repeaters. Switching to lower Spread factors reduces range and will delink our mesh at this stage.

messages.author: pacjunk
messages.text: Wide bandwidth is supposed to help with interference as it occupies a smaller part of the "chirp". Dodging the interference with narrow BW is great until something pops up on your frequency. Anyway the truth is in the pudding, and a lot have found narrow to work better. CR doesn't increase sensitivity, it just adds more error correction (and time).
messages.content_html: Wide bandwidth is supposed to help with interference as it occupies a smaller part of the "chirp". Dodging the interference with narrow BW is great until something pops up on your frequency. Anyway the truth is in the pudding, and a lot have found narrow to work better. CR doesn't increase sensitivity, it just adds more error correction (and time).

messages.author: Buzz
messages.text: Hey all I'm looking at buying t deck plus has anyone enjoyed their experience with this device and where did you purchase it from? Where was the best place to buy one I was looking at buying it off the lilygo website
messages.content_html: Hey all I'm looking at buying t deck plus has anyone enjoyed their experience with this device and where did you purchase it from? Where was the best place to buy one I was looking at buying it off the lilygo website

messages.author: pelgraine
messages.text: I have two of them - one 915 and one 433, both with the 3D printed back to fit a 6000mah battery as the battery life of the standard 2k one is less than I’d like. Depending on use a 6k battery can be 2-3 days. I like the fact it’s standalone and the new black keyboard is definitely easier to type on. With the bmorcelli launcher firmware you can run MeshCore, Meshtastic and any other tdeck compatible firmware you like on the one device
messages.content_html: I have two of them - one 915 and one 433, both with the 3D printed back to fit a 6000mah battery as the battery life of the standard 2k one is less than I’d like.
